cards.definition: An individual participation component (5% of grade) in which each student briefly pitches an original product idea to the class, developing concise communication and concept articulation skills.

Any misrepresentation with intent to deceive, failure to acknowledge sources, falsification of information, cheating at examinations, or inappropriate use of resources — all prohibited under NUS policy.
The long-term cumulative value embedded in a brand, built through consistent strategic choices and consumer associations over time.
The long-term vitality and consumer relevance of a brand, contrasted with short-term performance metrics. Decisions that boost immediate results must be balanced against sustaining brand health.
The practice of crafting and communicating a coherent narrative around a brand or product to build consumer connection and meaning; featured in the Week 4 guest speaker session.
The structured process of assessing new product ideas against market, technical, and financial criteria before committing to full development; covered in Week 3.

A quantitative research technique that measures how consumers trade off among product attributes to reveal preference structures and implied willingness to pay; used in NPD to optimize product design.
Quantitative and experimental methods for projecting consumer demand and market response before a product's full commercial launch; covered in Week 6.
A structured, human-centered approach to innovation emphasizing empathy with users, iterative prototyping, and creative problem-solving; exemplified by the IDEO case in Week 8.
The written deliverable of the group project, worth 20% of the final grade, documenting the full new product launch strategy developed by each team.
relatedTerms: Group Project on New Product Launching

cards.term: Group Project on New Product Launching
cards.definition: The primary applied assessment in MKT3717, comprising group presentations (25%) and a final marketing plan report (20%), requiring teams to develop and present a complete new product launch strategy.
An approach to product and service design that places end-user needs, behaviors, and experiences at the center of the innovation process, as opposed to technology- or operations-driven approaches.
The quantitative exercise of estimating the total addressable market (TAM) or relevant opportunity size for a new product; practiced as a hands-on exercise in Week 2.
The full end-to-end process by which firms move from identifying customer needs to designing, testing, launching, and managing new products; the organizing spine of the first half of the course.
A structured assessment component (5% of grade) in which students formally evaluate their group project teammates' contributions, reinforcing accountability in collaborative work.
relatedTerms: Group Project on New Product Launching

cards.definition: Taking someone else's work or ideas and presenting them as one's own; strictly prohibited under NUS academic integrity policy.
The scheme by which the functional elements of a product are arranged and interact; covered in Week 3 as part of concept development and prototyping.
The stages a product passes through from introduction through growth, maturity, and decline; the course's managerial perspective involves actively managing products across all these stages.

The activity of planning and organizing a service's components — people, infrastructure, communication, and material — to improve quality and user experience; featured in Week 4 and the Week 8 IDEO case.
